text: Hello, welcome on this video about laparoscopic total gastrectomy. My name is Benjamin Cadier. I'm a surgeon at St. Peter's Hospital in Brussels.

This is a case of a seventy-nine-year-old patient who suffered from epigastric pain. The worker found a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma from the lesser curvature without any signs of metastatic disease. He went through preoperative chemotherapy.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic total gastrectomy by dissecting the omentum at its colic insertion, using five trocars while the patient is positioned in the French position.

The surgeon carefully navigates the adhesions between the omentum and the mesocolon in a patient with significant adipose tissue, switching from active sealing to blunt dissection to identify the correct tissue plane and prevent injury to the mesocolon.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the omentum from its colic insertion, revealing the posterior gastric wall with visible tumor retractions. The dissection proceeds from left to right, exposing the head of the pancreas and station six lymph node, where the right gastroepiploic vein and artery are controlled.

The surgeon uses traction on the pylorus to aid in identifying the right gastroepiploic vein and artery in a fatty patient. Blunt dissection with a suction instrument is employed to delineate the upper limit of the head of the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to precisely dissect the right gastroepiploic artery, ensuring early division and control of the vessel during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the dissection of station six and places a gauze posterior to the pylorus. The anterior dissection is then performed, exposing station five. After fully dissecting the pylorus, a twelve millimeter trocar is inserted for stapling the first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the territory of the right gastric artery, controlling a small branch that extends directly to the duodenum. The procedure then advances to the hepatic hilum, where lymph node station twelve is identified and dissected alongside the hepatic artery.
The surgeon performs an early division of the right gastric artery, using clips to control lymphatic vessels to prevent lymphatic leakage.

The surgeon performs careful dissection alongside the portal vein using a coagulating hook. The dissection continues along the right gastric artery, removing lymph nodes from station five, and progresses to station eight alongside the hepatic artery.

The surgeon performs an upper dissection of the lesser omentum, targeting lymph node station one. The dissection continues to the left gastric artery and vein, progressing cephalically to lymph node station seven and then back to station one. The procedure advances to the upper left dissection of the stomach.

The surgeon carefully dissects the left gastroepiploic vein and artery, which are challenging to locate in fatigued patients. The gastrosplenic ligament is dissected with caution to avoid entering the splenic hilum, preventing potential bleeding. Major traction of the stomach to the right side exposes lymph node station two, completing the lymph node dissection. The last few centimeters of the esophagus are freed from adhesions to optimally expose the lower third for stapling.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the vascular supply from the esophagus, ensuring it is not carried too deeply into the thorax. The specimen is retrieved through a fan and steel incision. The angle of Treitz is identified, and the jejunum is lifted to perform the esophagojejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a blue cartridge stapler to perform a linear lateral anastomosis, with the assistant's trocar stretching the limb on the stapler to align the two sides for a minimal hole size. The anastomosis is then completed using two half running sutures with PDS 2-0.

The surgeon ties two half running sutures at the midpoint of the previous anterotomy. A 60-centimeter alimentary limb is measured, and a laterolateral jejuno-jejunal anastomosis is performed using a linear stapler with a white cartridge. The anterotomy is closed using two half running sutures.

The surgeon divides the ozojejunal and jejunojejunal anastomosis. The oestrogen anastomosis is fixed to the right cross using silk sutures. The twelve millimeter trocar port is closed, and a drainage tube is left in place.
